questions.scenario:
The DevPolaris release pipeline pushed a new orders API image, but production still reports healthy tasks from the previous task definition.

Image pushed is 111122223333.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/devpolaris-orders-api:2026-05-02.4. ECS service taskDefinition is devpolaris-orders-api:41. Task definition 41 image is ...:2026-05-02.3. Target health is 2 healthy tasks from revision 41.

What is missing from the runtime contract?

options.text: Force a new deployment of revision 41 because ECS will resolve the newly pushed tag even though the task definition names the old release tag.
The service has not been moved to a task definition revision that points at the new image.
Register a revision for the new image but leave the service on revision 41 until the old targets fail health checks.
Retag the new image as `2026-05-02.3` so revision 41 picks it up without changing the service record.

questions.explanation:
The image exists, but production still runs the old recipe. ECS serves traffic from the task definition revision used by the service, not from the newest tag in ECR. The runtime contract must connect artifact, revision, service, and health.

Learn more in [Amazon ECS rolling deployments](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/deployment-type-ecs.html).

questions.scenario:
The DevPolaris release captain is reviewing a production change and its recorded rollback evidence.

Release is orders-api-prod-2026-05-02.4. Approved image digest is sha256:8f3d2b9a. Task definition registered is devpolaris-orders-api:42.

Task definition 42 shows: Image is .../devpolaris-orders-api:latest.

What is the review concern?

options.text: Use the release tag but omit the resolved digest from the release record because repository tag immutability is enabled.
Keep `latest`, but record the repository digest at approval time and assume ECS resolves the same value during later deployments.
The task definition does not clearly pin or record the approved artifact, so production may not run the reviewed digest.
Pin the digest in a new task definition but approve it without comparing that digest to the reviewed release artifact.

questions.explanation:
A release record should let the team connect the reviewed artifact to the runtime recipe. Floating tags can move and make rollback or audit harder. Pinning or recording the exact digest keeps the release explainable.

Learn more in [Amazon ECR tag immutability](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECR/latest/userguide/image-tag-mutability.html).

questions.scenario:
A deployment reaches RUNNING tasks, but target health says:

```text
Target IP       Port   State       Reason
10.0.18.42      3000   unhealthy   Health checks failed with status code 404
10.0.27.113     3000   unhealthy   Health checks failed with status code 404
```

App routes in the release log: /healthz, /v1/orders

What should be fixed?

options.text: Change the target group success matcher to include 404 so the current route can pass without modifying the application.
Change the success-code matcher to include 404 so the existing `/health` response is considered ready.
The health contract: restore /health or update the target group to the readiness route the app serves.
Increase health-check grace time because a longer wait changes an existing `/health` 404 into `/healthz`.

questions.explanation:
The tasks are reachable enough to answer with 404. That is a health-path mismatch, not an image-pull failure. The ALB and app need one agreed readiness contract.

Learn more in [Application Load Balancer target health](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing/latest/application/target-group-health-checks.html).

questions.scenario:
The DevPolaris release captain must prove that production is running the reviewed artifact, not merely that an image exists in ECR. The captain collects this release evidence.

Service taskDefinition is devpolaris-orders-api:43. Task definition image digest is sha256:8f3d2b9a. CloudWatch startup log is release=2026-05-02.4. Target health is two healthy task IPs on port 3000.

Which evidence helps prove the running version?

options.text: The task definition points to the approved image digest or immutable artifact reference.
An ECR tag currently resolves to the approved digest, but the running task definition uses a different immutable reference.
The service is pointing to the expected task definition revision.
Startup logs from the new tasks report the expected release identifier.
The deployment reached `COMPLETED`, but startup logs from the replacement tasks do not include a release identifier.

questions.explanation:
Running-version proof needs a chain from service to recipe to artifact to task logs and health. A generic latest tag in ECR does not prove what the service is running. It is only useful if the runtime recipe points to the reviewed artifact.

Learn more in [Amazon ECR tag immutability](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECR/latest/userguide/image-tag-mutability.html).

questions.scenario: DevPolaris promoted orders API release `2026-05-05.2` at 09:12 UTC. Eight minutes later the ALB 5xx alarm is active, the ECS service has only two of three desired tasks running, and one registered target returns `Target.ResponseCodeMismatch`. A third task stopped with `ResourceInitializationError: unable to retrieve secret` and produced no startup log. The release captain must decide whether the image is faulty or the runtime contract failed. What is the best first investigation thread?

options.text: Inspect only task execution-role access because the initialization error makes target health and customer impact secondary.
Connect ALB target health, ECS task state, task definition secret references, and role permission before rebuilding the image.
Rollback immediately without confirming whether the previous revision remains compatible with current secrets and dependencies.
Inspect the secret permission in isolation and defer target-health and customer-impact evidence until the task starts.

questions.explanation:
The evidence crosses several runtime checks. The image may exist, while the service can still fail because health checks, task startup, or secret access are wrong. The first pass should connect AWS evidence instead of guessing from one alarm.

Learn more in [Amazon ECS task execution IAM role](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/task_execution_IAM_role.html).

questions.scenario: A new orders API rollout is causing checkout errors above the paging threshold. Task definition revision 57 still has one healthy target and was the verified production release before 09:12 UTC. Revision 58 is now primary, but it has one unhealthy target and one stopped task. IAM simulation confirms that revision 58's task execution role receives an implicit deny when ECS retrieves `payments-token` for secret injection. What is the best immediate action?

options.text: Keep revision 58 primary but lower the deployment maximum percent, hoping the remaining healthy revision 57 target absorbs traffic while new tasks retry.
Rotate the secret and force another revision 58 deployment before changing IAM, because a fresh secret might bypass the current access denial.
Leave both revisions running at the current weights while investigating, because one healthy target means the active customer impact can wait.
Stop the rollout pressure, restore traffic to revision 57, then fix the execution-role permission before retrying revision 58.

questions.explanation:
The new revision has a known runtime permission failure and customer impact is active. Returning to the known-good task definition protects users while the team fixes the permission path.

Learn more in [Amazon ECS task execution IAM role](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/task_execution_IAM_role.html).

questions.scenario:
A DevPolaris orders deployment has created running tasks, but none has earned traffic. The release captain compares artifact, capacity, health, and application evidence.

Task definition is devpolaris-orders-api:42. Image digest is sha256:8f3d. RunningCount is 2. ALB target health is 0 healthy, 2 unhealthy Target.ResponseCodeMismatch. CloudWatch logs is GET /health status=500 reason=config_not_ready.

Which contract part is false?

options.text: Health and runtime configuration, because the tasks exist but are not trusted to receive traffic.
The artifact contract is false because an existing digest cannot be trusted until health checks pass.
The traffic and readiness contract is false even though task count and artifact evidence look correct.
The capacity contract is false because two RUNNING tasks cannot be unhealthy at the same time.

questions.explanation:
Running tasks are not enough. The health endpoint is part of the traffic contract, and the log says config is blocking readiness. The artifact exists, but production still cannot safely send users to the new tasks.

Learn more in [Application Load Balancer target health](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing/latest/application/target-group-health-checks.html).
